speaker: interviewer
text: --Dunn. You were on your way to Europe with the 42nd--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Division.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I-- I think I was talking about the-- what we did during the three weeks we were at sea. Well, we were dodging U-boats. And-- and during the day, when we weren't resting or reading or something, I was with my particular troops. And we would-- we would talk about what's going to happen in combat. We had our exercises. We had to exercise. And we had classes. We didn't fire any weapons, but we went through book-wise what kinds of operations we might expect to be involved with. Obviously, in Europe, there was going to be fighting in cities. There would be combat in open field areas. And so we talked about that a lot, what we would expect. And we would have formations and lectures. And we saw films of news, news broadcasts [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on board ship, and tried to occupy ourselves really in not getting heavily involved in the combat situation, because we didn't want to get so antsy about it that we were all worn out before we got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So part of the time was relaxation, a lot of time in eating when we weren't seasick-- and I fortunately wasn't most of the trip-- and reading and we had movies. Every evening, there was a movie shown aboard. So it was a-- played cards. It was a relatively pleasant trip, except the stormy portion of it. And we never were attacked by any U-boats. Besides knowing that there's a military war going on, did you know that there was another war going on in Hitler's political party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within Germany, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, that Hitler was going in one direction and the other part of the country was going in another direction. And of course, that was, I'm sure, the start of his bullying the-- the people who didn't necessarily support him. Yes, I was aware of that happening. In fact, that was the burning of the books, the Reichstag, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about that amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean during this--

speaker: interviewer
text: During this journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this period of time? Didn't talk about that. You're talking now about aboard ship?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we really didn't, really-- the business was really not strategic. It was tactical. We talked about the tactical aspects of warfare, didn't really get into the big picture situation. We didn't even know what army we were going to be in when we got there. Turned out that we were in the Seventh Army and in Patton's Third Army part of the time. But we didn't get into any of the strategic aspects of the war or where we would be or any of that, just I guess more simplistic than being in the strategic discussions. It was more how we would fight a battle within our small contingent, company and battalion size maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what rank did you have at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a second lieutenant at the time, a platoon leader. I had-- my responsibility primarily was with about 40-odd privates and sergeants and corporals. That was-- I was the leader of the 3rd Platoon of Company A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you embark when you arrived to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through the-- into the Mediterranean Sea through the Straits of Gibraltar and landed in Marseilles, France, instead of-- we were-- went-- landed there, of course, much later than the Normandy invasion. And so we didn't really invade. We landed in Marseilles and we were trucked out to a-- ah, a god-awful place outside of Marseilles on the side of a hill. And we pitched our tents, and it rained and it rained and it rained. And it was muddy and miserable. We were-- I think they did that on purpose so that we would-- we would just relish getting into combat. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there about two weeks. And then we were ordered to load up on boxcars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And for six days, we were in these 40-and-8 boxcars. And we were just-- we were stacked so full-- so many people per car that all of us couldn't lie down at once, so we had to take turns lying down or sitting down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took us six days to get there. We got to Lyon, France, which was just a little east of Nancy. And we disembarked there and we were trucked to-- actually to Strasbourg.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in December of-- actually, we went into a fort on the Maginot Line initially. And then we were-- this was just before Christmas of 1944. As a matter of fact, we spent Christmas Eve and Christmas night in this fort, and we had a turkey dinner. It was-- it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We felt very strange, though. Here we are. We're in-- in-- in this place, but we're not in combat. And when are we going to be in combat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we only spent I think three days there. And then we were trucked to Strasbourg, France and quartered in a military school for about three more days, and then to a-- a chateau on the banks of the Rhine River. And our job was-- this is an absolutely gorgeous chateau. I think I showed you a booklet of it. And we stayed there about a week. And we would send patrols out to a-- it was actually a tower that we went up in to observe the enemy across the river. And every so often, we'd fire off a few rounds across the river. Never hit anybody, I don't think. And they'd do the same. The Germans across the river would do the same. And that was our initiation to combat. Not much fighting, really. It was just a-- it was kind of-- kind of exciting, though. You know, here, the enemy's right over there. He's across the river. And we're going to see him one of these days soon. Shortly thereafter, after about a week in this hacienda or this chateau, we went north to a-- a place called Schonenberg. And my particular job-- our company was assigned on what's called an outpost line. And an outpost line is a line beyond the main line of resistance. And our job was to try to disorganize the enemy, to make him believe that we were the main line and commit his forces. And so we did that. And as a matter of fact, I was-- my platoon-- I was a platoon leader then. My platoon was on the top of this hill. And we could look down in this valley. And this-- we had taken over the foxholes from another unit. And the night after we took over was the beginning in that particular sector of the Battle of the Bulge. And we were sitting there on a line, not armed well enough. We didn't have anti-tank weapons. And-- and so we were fighting off tanks with machine guns and rifles. And that's pretty hard to do with armored tanks. But I recall looking down this valley. And there was this tank with a bunch of German infantry following it. And one of my guys, it had to be 800 yards away from this tank and this infantry. He fired a shot and hit the last guy in line behind this tank. What a shot. [LAUGHS] But we spent three days on this line fighting off attacks by the Germans. One night, a tank came up on our position, and they didn't know where we were, and started firing his gun. And because he didn't know, he kind of backed off. We were surrounded, I'm told, five times by the German military. But our patrols would come up and break through, and they'd-- they'd retreat. And finally, we were given the order to come back to the main line, because they had it finally organized so that we didn't have to continue to fight. You're only supposed to be on an outpost for a few hours. You don't have the firepower that's back behind you. We were five miles in front of the main line. Well, we came back, and we got back at night and spent that night on the side of a hill. And it snowed and it snowed and it snowed. And fortunately, we had blankets and sleeping bags. And-- but I got up the next morning, I couldn't find anybody. Everybody was under snow. So I had to go around and help uncover my guys. And then we were moved from there and quartered in a fort on the Maginot Line, which was near a little town called Ingersheim. Our battalion was in a place called Schonenberg behind this town. And then we were in this fort out near-- out in front of-- of the little town of Ingersheim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we spent many an hour in this fort. And the rest of the time we would go out and dig foxholes to-- to prevent attacks by the Germans. If we-- an attack was pending, we'd go out and assume these foxholes that we'd dug. And one day, pretty traumatic. I was out there with my platoon. And one of the sergeants in our company was supervising the digging of these holes. And he was hit almost point blank by an 88 artillery round. Ooh. Sort of messed him up. And so we spent a good deal of time in that area, until it was determined that we were out on a-- on quite a salient. So we had to pull back. And this one-- one night, we pulled back and it was snowing. And the roads were icy and we had half-tracks and trucks slipping off the road. And we had to pull back about 25 miles to solidify the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of losses did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have too many. We had a few captured. The night we moved back from-- from this outpost, we-- I had three, four of the men in my company were captured. They got-- wandered off somewhere and got captured. We didn't have any-- any wounded on the outpost. During the patrols that were sent out, we lost probably a dozen out of our whole company. I didn't lose any people from my platoon during that period of time. Our job was-- was on a defensive position. We did a lot of patrolling, contact patrols with the unit-- units on either side, and would look for German patrols and what they were doing. So it was mostly patrolling. We didn't-- and on one of these patrols, we had a-- a number of folks from another platoon killed. In fact, one of the guys that I was quite friendly with who was a platoon leader was killed on one night. And it was a-- it didn't need to have happened. We had a-- a deputy battalion commander who was a-- a nut. And he sent out this patrol that shouldn't have been sent out and this fellow was killed. McLoren was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like seeing some of your friends losing their lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were like a family, you know? And one of our people would be killed and it was like-- like one of the members of the family was killed, like McLoren, who was killed on this patrol. I-- I almost went to this major, the deputy battalion commander, and-- and probably would have gotten court-martialed if I'd had done what I wanted to do, and that was to kill him. It was a senseless loss of life. And I felt very-- like a brother had been killed. We really had a hard time, knowing full well that that's-- you know, we're in combat. Somebody's going to get hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those four who were captured, did you ever hear from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they-- none of them-- they all survived and heard from-- well, three of them. One-- one died of pneumonia, I think, in captivity. The other three were repatriated by another unit late in the war. And they were all in fairly good shape. And I-- I met a couple of them on one of our division reunions that we have each year in July in the hottest places anybody can find. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the Battle of the Bulge, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We then were moved to another location, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still in-- in a defensive position. And this was in the Harz Mountains in Alsace-Lorraine. And we assumed these positions up in these mountains. And across the valley were the Germans. And they were occupying a portion of their sector in Alsace-Lorraine. We stayed there from January, middle of January, again patrolling, middle of January 'til the offensive in March of '45. And I've got a couple of funny stories that I'd like to tell about what happened during this, and I'll tell a traumatic story. Maybe I'll tell that first. I was told to take a patrol out. We were going to-- to go to a-- a city named Metz and join with another patrol and capture the town and hold it for a pending attack. And that would be a hub of the attack. We were supposed to get there and hold the town. Well, we didn't get very far. We got across the valley and went up the other side. And one of my guys stepped on a shoe mine and blew his foot off. And so I called back to say that, you know, we've run into a minefield and I don't think we can proceed. And the other unit was having trouble too. They ran into a terrible fight. And so we were both told to come back. We had minefields down in front of our positions. And I knew the pathway through the minefield. And I said, I want permission to come back through the minefield, because going-- coming back the same way we went out is pretty dangerous, because if somebody sees us coming out, they're going to wait and watch us come back. So I said, I want to come back through the minefield. And my request was denied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back the same route we came out. And sure enough, we're halfway across this valley, and a-- and a mortar round lights right in the middle of us, killed the guy that had his foot blown off, plus one of the stretcher-bearers. And I got shrapnel hit me in the neck, but didn't actually wound me. It knocked me down. But I had a big, heavy scarf on, and it-- so I-- the skin wasn't penetrated.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that-- that was awfully traumatic for me, to lose one of my guys. And if they'd had listened to me and allowed me to do what I wanted to do, it probably wouldn't have happened. So I-- I was pretty angry about that. One of the funny stories is I'm sitting in my foxhole listening on the radio, because I've got one of my-- two of my guys down on the very front of our unit and they're at-- on listening posts. And this one fella, his name was Autrey. He's a hillbilly from Tennessee, a crack shot. He's down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hear these explosions. And I said, Autrey, what's going on? He says, Lieutenant, there's Krauts out here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, throw out some grenades, Autrey. He said, lieutenant, I done throwed three out. They done throwed four back. And I said, Autrey, OK. I'll-- we'll be right down. By the time I got down there, the Germans had left. The other funny story is one of my other guys, he was a real screw-up in the States. His name was Jimmy Kitchens. And-- but overseas, he was an outstanding soldier, just outstanding. And he happened to be down on one of these listening posts. This one night, the Germans must have gotten a trainload of ammunition, because they were throwing artillery rounds at us, just volley after volley after volley. And so it's 2 o'clock in the morning. We're all hunkered down in our bunkers and we're not getting hurt at all. But we have this company commander who's a very nervous type. He-- big football player from Texas. And so he says-- and we-- our code names were-- Able 6 was the company commander. Able 1 was the first platoon leader, Able 2 and 3 and 4, and Able 5 was the-- the executive officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, Loomis was his name, Captain Loomis. And so this flock of ammunition comes pouring into our unit. And he says, how many was that? How many? This is Able-- this is Able 6. How many was that? And Jimmy Kitchens is down on the listening post. He says, sir, I believe that was six rounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this went on all the time. You know, we're trying to be relaxed and sleep if possible. And-- and he's continuing this tirade of, how many rounds was that, because he's getting pretty excited about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, this bunch of rounds comes in. And he says, this is Able 6. How many was that? And Kitchens says, this is Able Kitchens. That was 999 rounds and we're all dead. [LAUGHS] And Loomis says, god damn it Kitchens, this is serious business, serious business. [LAUGHS] So those are-- those are-- the things you had to have in combat to keep you sane, to have these funny kind of things go on. And we just-- all of us in the whole company just came unglued when Kitchens did that, all but Loomis. Did you have any entertainment in-- in the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: During combat? Not really very much. Most of it was eating and sleeping and trying not to get killed. Not-- not much, some reading. Of course, we were in foxholes. We did get leaves. In fact, I-- I got to go back to Nancy on a leave for a week. And that was a pleasant experience. But other than that, we-- we were all business, patrolling and-- and trying to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Nancy, for example, did you mingle with the German population? Was there any German--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: French population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, French. Yeah. Yeah. We were-- we had liberated-- France was pretty much liberated by then, except part of Alsace-Lorraine, where we were. Yeah. I mingled with the-- with French people. And I became friends of a lieutenant, an Air Force lieutenant, Air Corps in those days, Army Air Corps. And we kind of palled around while we were there. We lived in a hotel. And just it was a very restful, pleasant experience to go back there. Of course, I had to go-- go back to the front again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any French units work with you, fight with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yeah. Well, not-- didn't mingle with. We had a French unit on our immediate right of our regiment. And they were crazy. [LAUGHS] Every night they'd fire off ammunition and just like a fireworks going off. Whether they were hitting anybody or not, I don't know. But they were crazy guys. But they were-- they were really brave, brave fighters, good fighters, and respected them a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- well, after our-- our defensive-- completion of our defensive work, on March 15th, I guess it was, we set off on a-- the attack that was going to end-- end in the war hopefully. And we-- we moved from our positions to the attack positions and took off in the wee hours and got across this valley and started moving up the mountains. And we came under intense enemy fire and pinned us down. And we were on the side of a hill and machine gun bullets were hitting, hitting us. And fortunately, my platoon didn't suffer any casualties from these guns that were firing from across the way. It wasn't 'til later that we lost some folks. But I-- I guess it's funny how you become what-- in some people's minds you're a hero. I didn't think of myself as a hero at all at the time. But I asked my bazooka man to fire off a shot down-- I could see these bunkers that we were getting fire from. And he fired off a shot and it hit near one of these bunkers. And I saw these Germans jump out of the bunker and go run down to another place. And I said, let's go. So I got up and all my troops and we ran down this hill and captured that particular area. We caught, oh, about 15 German prisoners. And for that, I was awarded the Silver Star. I guess you saw that thing in the book. You know, I didn't think of myself as a hero at all or anybody that should have been awarded a medal, because I was getting tired of sitting on the side of this hill being shot at. And if there's a chance that I can capture them before they get ready, I should do it. Get up and go. This isn't winning a war, sitting here on the side of a hill. So we went down and captured these folks. And then we started to proceed and we ran into some snipers that-- that killed one of my machine gunners and his-- his ammunition bearer. And so we neutralized them and then really started our move toward the Siegfried Line. And that was an interesting situation, going up these steep mountains, in the Harz range of mountains. We were so tired by the time we got halfway up these mountains that we'd hardly move. But we would look across and see these poor people in the heavy weapons company carrying their heavy base plates and their mortar tubes and their heavy machine guns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it gave us a little more restful feeling and we were able to negotiate. It was so steep we couldn't even take our mules, pack mules, up. We had to carry everything that we wanted, our food and our ammunition, had to carry them up. And we had had pack mules to carry the heavy ammunition and food and other things, but they couldn't even get up the mountains it was so steep.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in combat, were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. All the time. But you know, I got a job. I'm scared to death. Everybody's scared to death. You know, you face-- face death and you're afraid. And I was. But I wasn't so afraid that I couldn't function. As a matter of fact, I felt a real responsibility. I'm-- I'm their leader. I'm the platoon leader, and then later I was the company commander. I'm responsible for these guys. And if I don't tuck my fear to the point where I can function, I'm no good. And so I was able to lead them well.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop to change tapes, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
